content.problemStatement:
You are given an array A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ consisting of NNN integers. Your goal is to make the GCD of all the elements in the array an odd integer. To achieve this goal, you can do the following operation any number of times:


Choose an index i  (1≤i≤N)i\;(1\leq i \leq N)i(1≤i≤N) such that Ai>1A_i \gt 1Ai​>1 and set Ai=⌊Ai2 ⌋A_i = \lfloor \frac{A_i}{2}\ \rfloorAi​=⌊2Ai​​ ⌋
You can choose an index multiple times during the operations. Find the minimum number of operations after which GCD of all the elements in the array becomes an odd integer.

**Note:** ⌊x⌋\lfloor x \rfloor⌊x⌋ : Returns the largest integer that is less than or equal to xxx (i.e  rounds down to the nearest integer). For example, ⌊1.5⌋=1,⌊2⌋=2,\lfloor 1.5 \rfloor = 1, \lfloor 2 \rfloor = 2,⌊1.5⌋=1,⌊2⌋=2, ⌊72 ⌋\lfloor \frac{7}{2}\ \rfloor⌊27​ ⌋   =⌊3.5⌋\;= \lfloor 3.5 \rfloor=⌊3.5⌋   =3\;= 3=3.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer - the minimum number of operations after which GCD of all the elements in the array becomes an odd integer.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 5·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N, the size of the array. The next line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the minimum number of operations required so that the GCD of all array elements becomes odd.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: array already contains odd numbers, so 0 operations.\nTest case 2: 6 needs one division (6→3) to become odd, minimum is 1.\nTest case 3: the smallest trailing‑zero count among {4,12,24} is 2 (4→2→1), so answer is 2.

hidden.label: all evens with same trailing zeros

solution.approach: The GCD of the whole array is odd iff at least one element is odd. For any element Ai we can repeatedly replace it by floor(Ai/2), which is equivalent to removing one factor of 2 from its prime factorisation each time. The number of operations needed to turn Ai into an odd number equals the exponent of 2 in Ai (the count of trailing zeros in its binary representation). Therefore the answer for a test case is the minimum such exponent over all array elements. We compute this by iterating over the array, counting trailing zeros for each Ai (using a loop that right‑shifts while the number is even), and keeping the smallest count.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over the whole input.
